heading: What This Column Does
body: **Negation** flips a truth value. If a base variable is true, its negation is false. If a base variable is false, its negation is true.

examples.text: If $P$ is T, then $\sim P$ is F.
If $P$ is F, then $\sim P$ is T.

note: This lesson covers only direct negation of a single base variable.

heading: Copy Then Flip
body: To build the column for **direct negation**, copy the rows of the base variable and reverse each value one by one. Do not change the row order.

examples.text: From $P$: `T T F F`, build $\sim P$: `F F T T`.
From $P$: `T T F F`, build $\sim P$: `T T F F`.

heading: Single Column Example
body: Place the $\sim P$ column next to the $P$ column. Then flip each row directly across.
note: Each row in $\sim P$ depends only on the matching row in $P$.

heading: Use Base Rows Only
body: **Base variable** means an original propositional letter such as $P$ or $Q$. When a formula contains $\sim P$, first build the base column for $P$, then derive $\sim P$ from it.

examples.text: To evaluate $(\sim P \vee Q)$, first write columns for $P$ and $Q$.
To evaluate $(\sim P \vee Q)$, build $\sim P$ before writing $P$.

heading: Do Not Reorder
body: A common mistake is rearranging rows while filling the negation column. The truth table order stays fixed. Only the truth values flip.

examples.text: If $P$ is `T F T F`, then $\sim P$ is `F T F T`.
If $P$ is `T F T F`, then $\sim P$ is `T F F T`.

note: Negation changes values, not row positions.

heading: Core Method
body: Build the base-variable column first. Then make the negated column by flipping each truth value in the same row: T becomes F, and F becomes T.
